channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: ‘Ragtime’ Star Joshua Henry Is Bringing Broadway to Its Feet
item.description: Joshua Henry, a Tony nominee for his thrilling performance in the musical, credits the demanding role with helping him “feel like I have mastered the voice.”
item.dc:creator: Brittani Samuel and Vincent Tullo

item.title: Addiction Counselor Who Helped Supply Ketamine to Matthew Perry Sentenced to Two Years
item.description: Erik Fleming, a licensed drug addiction counselor, had admitted to selling 51 vials of ketamine to the actor, including the dose that killed him.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:description: Matthew Perry, who played Chandler Bing on the 1990s sitcom “Friends,” publicly struggled with drug and alcohol addiction for decades.

item.title: ‘They Shooting at Us’: Rihanna and ASAP Rocky Outlined Attack to Police
item.description: The singers discussed their stalkers and their experience inside a trailer struck by gunfire, according to a police report. A woman has been charged with attempted murder.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rihanna and ASAP Rocky at the Met Gala last week, about two months after a shooting at her home.

item.title: Can Three Auction Houses Sell $2.6 Billion Worth of Art in One Week?
item.description: Five luxury artworks hold the key to the spring season, one of the most anticipated sales in years. Major buyers are looking past female and younger artists and toward tradition.
item.dc:creator: Tim F. Schneider, Julia Halperin and Zachary Small

item.title: Mel Brooks Donates His Archives to the National Comedy Center
item.description: The comedian is contributing some 20,000 documents and photos covering his career, including his time in the Army and the lyrics for “Springtime for Hitler.”
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman

item.title: Jade Manns Is Breathing New Life Into Dance
item.description: Jade Manns is part of a generation of young dance artists who dig deep inside the body’s inner landscape. Nature leads the way.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas and Erik Tanner
item.media:credit: Erik Tanner for The New York Times

item.title: Cy Twombly, From Intimate Angles
item.description: In photos by his wife, whose negatives were discovered by his granddaughter, we see more personal sides to the 20th-century master.
item.dc:creator: Arthur Lubow

item.title: At Cannes 2026, Gaza Becomes a Political Flashpoint
item.description: The event’s leaders immediately jumped into the debate over whether festival participants should speak out on issues like Israel and Gaza.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:description: From left, the Cannes Film Festival president, Iris Knobloch, the jury president, Peter Jackson, and the festival director, Thierry Frémaux.

item.title: At Netflix Is a Joke, the Attention Economy Rules the Festival
item.description: Though stand-up by stars like John Mulaney and Dave Chappelle was on the program, roasts, podcasts and other shows that incorporated comedy were hot.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman

item.title: In Cleveland, Smokers Are Helping to Keep the Arts Alive
item.description: A novel cigarette tax has generated $270 million for cultural organizations in the Cleveland area, which makes declining smoking rates “a double-edged sword.”
item.dc:creator: Michaela Towfighi and Ross Mantle
item.category: Rock and Roll Hall of Fame and Museum
item.media:description: Playhouse Square, in downtown Cleveland, has used grants from a cigarette tax to explore creating a designated theater district akin to Times Square or the West End.

item.title: ‘A New Leaf’: A Romantic Comedy, With Murder on the Mind
item.description: This 1971 movie, restored and revived for a week at the IFC Center, offers ample evidence of Elaine May’s screwball genius.
item.dc:creator: J. Hoberman

item.title: Watching ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’ as an Elegy for Magazines
item.description: The film may be about fashion, but for some editors and writers, it gets a lot right about the dire state of fashion journalism.
item.dc:creator: Clarissa Cruz
item.media:description: Meryl Streep, left, and Anne Hathaway as fashion magazine editors dealing with a crumbling media landscape in “The Devil Wears Prada 2.”

item.title: Luna Lab Is Building a Future for Female Composers
item.description: Missy Mazzoli and Ellen Reid didn’t have female mentors. They founded Luna Composition Lab so young composers would.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Hirsch
item.media:description: Ellen Reid, left, and Missy Mazzoli: the two composers who founded Luna Composition Lab in New York.

item.title: In ‘Should I Marry a Murderer’ and ‘Trust Me,’ Informants Risk Almost Everything
item.description: In two new Netflix docuseries, women put their lives, safety and sanity on the line to help law enforcement bring down the bad guy.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam
item.category: Should I Marry a Murderer (TV Program)
Trust Me: The False Prophet (TV Program)
Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
item.media:description: Christine Marie, who spent years collecting evidence to help bring down a child sex predator, in the docuseries “Trust Me: The False Prophet.”

item.title: Stephen Colbert Slams ‘Trump’s Fabulous Billionaire Boys Trip’
item.description: Late night hosts responded to President Trump’s arrival in Beijing on Wednesday, accompanied by 17 chief executives including Tim Cook and Elon Musk.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Shakira, Madonna and BTS Are First World Cup Final Halftime Show
item.description: The previous World Cup final drew more than 500 million live viewers. This year’s matchup is scheduled for July 19 at MetLife Stadium.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:description: Shakira recorded the official World Cup song, “Dai Dai,” which features the Afrobeats star Burna Boy.

item.title: Ye Must Pay Musicians for Using Sample Without Permission
item.description: A jury in Los Angeles found the rapper and three of his companies liable for more than $400,000 after he played an unauthorized music sample at an event in 2021.
item.dc:creator: Michaela Towfighi
item.media:description: A spokesman for Ye called the verdict a “failed shakedown.”

item.title: Cynthia Shange, Who Defied Apartheid at a Beauty Pageant, Dies at 76
item.description: She was the first Black South African to enter the Miss World contest, placing in the top five in 1972.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:description: Cynthia Shange competing at a Miss World event in London in 1972. South Africa’s president praised the boldness of her ambitions “in an era when apartheid segregation was at its peak.”

item.title: Koji Suzuki, Sometimes Called the Stephen King of Japan, Dies at 68
item.description: His “Ring” trilogy helped create a genre known as J-horror and spawned a multimedia franchise, including one of the highest-grossing horror films ever made.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman
item.media:credit: Jennifer S. Altman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Koji Suzuki in 2004. Although his horror fiction made him wealthy, he was not a fan of the genre: “I really dislike most horror writing,” he told The New York Times.

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Joy to Your Day
item.description: Voice memos, snail mail and your own private screening room.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Are These the Bones of the Fourth Musketeer? This Dutch Village Hopes So.
item.description: Wolder, near the Belgian border, is waiting to see if the skeleton it dug up in a church is Count d’Artagnan, from Alexandre Dumas’s tale.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek
item.category: Artagnan, Chas de Baatz, Castelmore, D'
item.media:description: An excavation in St. Peter and Paul’s Church in Wolder, the Netherlands, to see if remains found in the church belong to Count d’Artagnan, who was dramatized, fictionalized and immortalized in Dumas’s “The Three Musketeers.”

item.title: ‘The A List’ Review: The Diaspora, Described
item.description: This documentary spotlights the individual histories and struggles of 15 figures from all walks of life, connected by their inclusion in the Asian and Pacific diasporas.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: Matt Dillon’s Lesser-Known Life as a Visual Artist
item.description: The actor’s large scale, Neo-Expressionist-inspired paintings are now on view in New York.
item.dc:creator: Max Lakin

item.title: Closed-Door Romance Books That Will Make You Swoon
item.description: The best-selling author Lynn Painter recommends low-heat love stories that deliver on juicy tension, crackling banter and plenty of swoon.
item.dc:creator: Lynn Painter

item.title: Jordan Klepper Wants the President to Get More Sleep
item.description: “Well, well, well, looks like the Sleepy Joe-er has become the sleepy Joe-ee,” Klepper said after President Trump was seen with his eyes closed during an event in the Oval Office.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Claire Maurier, the Narcissistic Mother in ‘400 Blows,’ Dies at 97
item.description: A landmark of New Wave cinema, “The 400 Blows” brought her enduring global attention. She later appeared in “La Cage Aux Folles” and “Amélie.”
item.dc:creator: Clyde Haberman
item.media:description: Claire Maurier as a bistro owner in “Amélie.” A whimsical comedy-drama-romance, it was a major commercial success in France and one of the highest-grossing French films ever released in the United States.

item.title: Drawings Dominate at a Chicago de Kooning Exhibit
item.description: In an exception to the usual paintings-heavy blockbuster exhibitions, the Art Institute of Chicago is presenting the masterful drawings of Willem de Kooning.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos

item.title: In Tuscany, an Artistic Sculpture Hub Thrives
item.description: Pietrasanta, in the Tuscan region of Italy, has become an international sculpture hub that bursts with activity, especially in summer.
item.dc:creator: Farah Nayeri
item.media:credit: Emanuele Camerini for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Armenian-born artist Mikayel Ohanjanyan at his studio. Seven of his sculptures are currently on display in Pietrasanta.

item.title: A Photographer of Newark’s People Gets a Show Among the People
item.description: A number of works by Manuel Acevedo are being displayed throughout the city in outdoor spots highly visible to the residents who inspired them.
item.dc:creator: Tariro Mzezewa
item.category: Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey

item.title: The Swiss Collector Building a Massive Trove of Chinese Art
item.description: Over three decades, Sigg, a Swiss businessman and former diplomat, amassed thousands of contemporary Chinese works. Ai Weiwei calls him “my maker.”
item.dc:creator: Mercedes Hutton
item.media:credit: Clara Watt for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Swiss art collector Uli Sigg at his home, near Lucerne, Switzerland. He amassed a huge trove of contemporary Chinese art, much of which he has donated to M+ museum in Hong Kong.

item.title: Spotlighting the Woman Who Brought European Modernism to California
item.description: Although Galka Scheyer might be an unfamiliar name, the artists she championed have become famous. An exhibition in Pasadena brings her to the foreground.
item.dc:creator: Will Higginbotham

item.title: Officers Sue Affleck and Damon’s Company Over ‘The Rip’ Portrayal
item.description: Two Miami law enforcement officials say the Netflix crime drama uses real-life details and portrays them in a negative light.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin

item.title: Amor Towles Shares Insights on His Own Rare Book Collection
item.description: The best-selling author and collector explains what draws him to add to his own bookshelves.
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Dulany
item.media:credit: Katherine Marks for The New York Times
item.media:description: Amor Towles has a collection of about 100 rare books at his Manhattan home in a mahogany-paneled room, decorated with antique and modern paintings and prints.

item.title: Revolution is the Theme at the Firsts London Book Fair
item.description: What to expect at the book fair in the Saatchi Gallery.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn

item.title: How to See Rare Books in London
item.description: Here’s a guide to illuminated manuscripts, antique tomes and first editions around the British capital.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Jeremie Souteyrat for The New York Times
item.media:description: The British Library’s collection of more than 170 million items includes more than 13.5 million printed books and e-books.

item.title: The Rolling Stones, Very Early and Very Late
item.description: Listen to their 1963 debut through two tunes released just last week.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz

item.title: Leigh Magar, High-End Milliner Turned Indigo Artist, Dies at 57
item.description: She was known for bespoke hats that attracted a celebrity following. Then she left Charleston for an island off South Carolina, where she began cultivating indigo.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green
item.media:credit: Candace Dane Chambers for The New York Times

item.title: Our Favorite Moments From This Year’s Tony-Nominated Broadway Shows
item.description: Here are some of the brilliant moments our writers can’t shake from this year’s batch of Tony-nominated productions.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw, Jesse Green, Laura Collins-Hughes, Elisabeth Vincentelli and Sara Krulwich
item.category: Two Strangers (Carry a Cake Across New York) (Play)

item.title: ‘Paranormal Activity’ to Haunt Broadway This Summer
item.description: Inspired by the popular film series, the play has already run in Britain and in four U.S. cities. It arrives in New York in August after a Boston stop.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.category: Paranormal Activity: A New Story Live on Broadway (Play)
item.media:credit: Evan Jenkins for The New York Times
item.media:description: The play, billed as a modern ghost story, had its first American stop in Chicago last fall.

item.title: Rex Reed, Film Critic Known for Acerbic Reviews, Dies at 87
item.description: He fawned over Old Hollywood stars and sparred with Frank Sinatra. Nora Ephron marveled at his ability to get his subjects to say the things they did.
item.dc:creator: Clyde Haberman
item.media:credit: Vincent Tullo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rex Reed in 2017. If he disliked someone or — worse — found the person merely uninteresting, it was wise to duck and cover.

item.title: A Reporter Shares His Secrets to Covering the Cannes Film Festival
item.description: Kyle Buchanan, who has attended the annual launchpad for Oscar contenders in France since 2013, subsists on Red Bull and around four hours of sleep.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:description: The red carpet at the 2025 Cannes Film Festival. Many movies screened during the festival go on to earn Academy Award nominations.

item.title: Stephen Colbert Hosts 4 of His ‘Best Television Friends’
item.description: Jimmy Kimmel, Jimmy Fallon, John Oliver and Seth Meyers, co-hosts of the Strike Force Five podcast along with Colbert, joined as guests on what is likely their last appearance together on the “Late Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: At Frieze New York and Beyond, Indigenous Artists Are in the Spotlight
item.description: With works showing in New York, Venice and elsewhere, Sara Flores, Suzanne Kite and Seba Calfuqueo are exploding past notions of what, exactly, Indigenous art is.
item.dc:creator: Nazanin Lankarani

item.title: How Much Art Is Too Much? A Guide to the New York Fairs.
item.description: Latin American galleries. World-class photography. Upcycled design. Art fairs will take over New York this week. Here are seven (of many) worth a visit.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman

item.title: Dudamel Unites N.Y. Philharmonic and Spanish Harlem Orchestra
item.description: Gustavo Dudamel brought the New York Philharmonic and the Spanish Harlem Orchestra together for the first time, at Lincoln Center and uptown.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney and James Estrin
item.category: Loew's 175th Street Theater/United Palace of Cultural Arts (Manhattan, NY)
item.media:description: Gustavo Dudamel congratulating the percussionist Luis Quintero, from the Spanish Harlem Orchestra, during a performance with the New York Philharmonic last week.

item.title: Kevin Hart Roast: Highlights From Tom Brady, the Rock, Katt Williams and More
item.description: Tom Brady, the Rock, Chelsea Handler and even Hart’s longtime rival Katt Williams told jokes at Hart’s expense at a Netflix roast.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:description: Actor and comedian Kevin Hart sat through many jokes.

item.title: Cannes Film Festival Preview: 4 Story Lines to Watch for
item.description: The lineup isn’t very starry, but changes in the Oscar rules could affect the Palme d’Or choice. That’s one development we’re tracking.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:description: Pedro Almodóvar at Cannes in 2023. He’s returning with “Bitter Christmas.”

item.title: The Dance Quotes and Callbacks in ‘Schmigadoon’ and ‘The Jellicle Ball’
item.description: Breaking down the choreographic allusions in “Schmigadoon!” and “Cats: The Jellicle Ball,” two gotta-dance, Tony-nominated shows.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert

item.title: In This Nazi-Era Restitution Dispute, the Focus Turns to a Missing Cow
item.description: A family is vying for the return of a painting it thought was by Rubens. But an expert says it’s a copy because it does not include the artist’s depiction of a peeing cow.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Hickley

item.title: ‘The Comeback’ Knows What Scares Hollywood
item.description: The HBO comedy, which wrapped up on Sunday, often seemed to be about whatever was keeping Lisa Kudrow and her collaborators up at night. But Valerie Cherish remained undaunted.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik and Alissa Wilkinson
item.media:description: Lance Barber, who played the obnoxious writer Paulie G. in the first season, briefly returned in the final season.

item.title: Amitav Ghosh Brings the Main Character of ‘Ghost Eye’ to Life, With the Help of a Sketch Artist
item.description: With the help of a forensic artist, Amitav Ghosh puts a face to the name of Varsha Gupta, the central figure of his new novel, “Ghost Eye.”
item.dc:creator: Jenny Comita

item.title: Lost Movie Music? On CD? La-La Land Is an Anomaly. (And a Success.)
item.description: The California record label scours movie studio archives and composers’ personal collections to locate music from older films or TV recordings.
item.dc:creator: Brian Raftery
item.media:credit: Alex Welsh for The New York Times
item.media:description: Matt Verboys (center), one of the founders of La-La Land Records, at work at the label’s offices in Burbank, Calif.

item.title: London Rooftop Where Beatles Played Last Gig to Become Museum
item.description: The townhouse at 3 Savile Row, where the band also recorded “Let It Be,” will open to the public next year, the group’s record label said.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: The Beatles playing their final performance on the roof of 3 Savile Row in London in 1969.

item.title: ‘Off-Campus,’ and 8 Things to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: A TV adaptation of a new hockey romance series. And a comedy series starring two “One Tree Hill” alums finds a new home.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:description: From left: Antonio Cipriano, Stephen Kalyn, Jalen Thomas Brooks and Belmont Cameli in “Off-Campus.”

item.title: ‘The Comeback’ Season 3 Finale Recap: All’s Well?
item.description: Valerie makes the case that her tale is one of survival, not humiliation. “You’ve been telling the wrong story,” she tells Jane.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.media:description: Despite the tendency for some people around Valerie (Lisa Kudrow) to view her as a doormat, she has always seen every painful moment as a step forward.

item.title: Maya Lin Connects Nature to a New Manhattan Skyscraper and Beyond
item.description: The famed artist and designer drew on her lifelong love of the environment for inspiration for new work in New York and Chicago.
item.dc:creator: Jane L. Levere
item.media:credit: Shuran Huang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lin’s new work at the JPMorgan Chase building in New York, a sculpture composed on massive gray stone walls, and the adjoining plaza were envisioned in part as a respite from the cacophony of the city.

item.title: Review: ‘The Receptionist’ Can’t Help You Today
item.description: A stale revival of Adam Bock’s cog-in-the-office-machine dark comedy lacks specificity and bite.
item.dc:creator: Brittani Samuel
item.media:description: Katie Finneran, left, and Mallori Johnson in “The Receptionist” at The Pershing Square Signature Center in Manhattan.

item.title: New Books Provide Divergent Views of the Art Market
item.description: Three new books — a sweeping work of nonfiction, a cheeky memoir and a dual biography — provide divergent views on the business of buying and selling, and they are out just in time for New York Art Week.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos

item.title: Michael Joo Steps Into the Art World’s Spotlight
item.description: Michael Joo is appearing in multiple shows, from New York and the San Francisco Bay Area to a Mediterranean island and Venice.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos
item.media:credit: Oliver Farshi for The New York Times

item.title: A Monument to Chocolate Is Wrapped in Layers of Mexican History
item.description: Combining a colonial house and a contemporary addition, the new Museum of Cacao & Chocolate sits on a grisly Aztec souvenir.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Gallow
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Huei, or great Tzompantli. It displays hundreds of human skulls belonging to people who were believed to have been sacrificed in the 15th century.

item.title: On ‘S.N.L.’, Brett Kavanaugh, Kash Patel and Pete Hegseth Walk Into a Bar
item.description: Matt Damon and Aziz Ansari return to roles they have played on “Saturday Night Live” alongside Colin Jost, in an episode hosted by Damon.
item.dc:creator: Dave Itzkoff

item.title: If You See Only 6 Venice Shows, Make It These
item.description: Thought-provoking and senses-stirring works on war, art patronage, and the Black experience in America are just some of our critics’ picks.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago, Blake Gopnik and Aruna D’Souza
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ashfika Rahman’s “Than Para — No Land Without Us” (2025-ongoing) in the “Still Joy” group exhibition at the Pinchuk Art Center.

item.title: ‘The Lost Boys’ Helped Define the ’80s in More Ways Than One
item.description: The film that’s been adapted into a new musical created a memorable aesthetic with its cast and outfits. Its ideas about the nuclear family also made it a movie for its time.
item.dc:creator: Maya Phillips
item.media:description: From left, Billy Wirth, Kiefer Sutherland, Brooke McCarter and Alex Winter in “The Lost Boys.”
